title: CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis

structured.summary: The paper presents CODEGEN, an open-source family of large-scale causal language models trained on extensive natural language and code corpora, demonstrating strong multi-turn program synthesis abilities. It proposes a multi-turn synthesis paradigm that decomposes complex programming tasks into simpler natural language prompts over multiple interactions and introduces the Multi-Turn Programming Benchmark (MTPB) to evaluate this capability. Experimental results show that multi-turn prompting improves synthesis performance compared to single-turn approaches, especially on medium and hard problems, with model performance scaling with size and domain-specific training data.

strengths.evidence: The paper formalizes program synthesis as an interactive multi-step process with natural language subproblems and provides a novel benchmark (MTPB) specifically designed to measure multi-turn synthesis capabilities.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results and scaling analysis
strengths.evidence: The largest CODEGEN-MONO (16.1B) matches or surpasses OpenAI Codex 12B on HumanEval, and multi-turn prompting yields significant pass rate improvements on the MTPB benchmark, especially for complex problems.
Release of CODEGEN model checkpoints and the JAXFORMER training framework lowers the barrier for reproducibility and further research in large code LLMs and multi-turn program synthesis.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient situating within related interactive/conversational program synthesis work
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks discussion and comparison to prior literature on iterative or dialog-based program synthesis approaches and specification refinement.

weaknesses.point: Limited ablation and analysis of training data impact and prompt length effects
weaknesses.evidence: No ablations isolate contributions of different datasets (THEPILE, BIGQUERY, BIGPYTHON) to multi-turn performance, nor controlled comparisons of multi-turn versus single-turn with matched total prompt token length.

weaknesses.point: Small and manually decomposed multi-turn benchmark
weaknesses.evidence: MTPB contains only 115 problems with 3+ turns, with multi-turn prompts manually created by authors rather than sourced from real user interactions, limiting diversity and generalizability.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility gaps in training and prompt formatting details
weaknesses.evidence: The paper omits full hyperparameter settings, training schedules, and explicit multi-turn prompt formatting templates, making exact replication challenging.

weaknesses.point: Lack of human factors evaluation and real user interaction data
weaknesses.evidence: No user studies or human-in-the-loop experiments validate that multi-turn specification improves usability or aligns with natural programmer workflows.

questions.question: How does the multi-turn prompting approach compare to prior interactive or conversational program synthesis systems in terms of effectiveness and usability?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify the true novelty and practical advantages of the proposed paradigm.

questions.question: What is the relative contribution of different training datasets and the presence of interleaved natural language comments to the emergence of multi-turn capabilities?
questions.motivation: Identifying key data sources can inform more targeted training and dataset curation for improved multi-turn program synthesis.

questions.question: Can multi-turn synthesis gains be sustained beyond the 16B parameter scale and for languages other than Python?
questions.motivation: Exploring this would assess the broader applicability and scalability of the approach.

questions.question: How does multi-turn prompting perform when prompts are collected from real-world users or conversational logs rather than author-decomposed specifications?
questions.motivation: Evaluating on real user data is critical to establish generalization and deployment readiness.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient situating within related interactive/conversational program synthesis work. The paper lacks discussion and comparison to prior literature on iterative or dialog-based program synthesis approaches and specification refinement
critique_points.point_002: Limited ablation and analysis of training data impact and prompt length effects. No ablations isolate contributions of different datasets (THEPILE, BIGQUERY, BIGPYTHON) to multi-turn performance, nor controlled comparisons of multi-turn versus single-turn with matched total prompt token length
critique_points.point_003: Small and manually decomposed multi-turn benchmark. MTPB contains only 115 problems with 3+ turns, with multi-turn prompts manually created by authors rather than sourced from real user interactions, limiting diversity and generalizability
critique_points.point_004: Reproducibility gaps in training and prompt formatting details. The paper omits full hyperparameter settings, training schedules, and explicit multi-turn prompt formatting templates, making exact replication challenging
critique_points.point_005: Lack of human factors evaluation and real user interaction data. No user studies or human-in-the-loop experiments validate that multi-turn specification improves usability or aligns with natural programmer workflows
